nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated and non-migratory. Juveniles are commonly found in tide pools and intertidal areas, while adults inhabit shallow coral and rocky reefs, typically at depths of 0–10 m and occasionally down to about 20–40 m.

diet.notes: The Sergeant Major accepts a wide variety of meaty and herbivorous foods in captivity, including flake, pellet, frozen, and freeze-dried preparations. In the wild it has been documented feeding on algae, zooplankton (copepods and shrimp larvae), invertebrate larvae, tunicates, small crustaceans, and small fishes; juveniles have also been observed cleaning parasites from sea turtles.

summary: A hardy Atlantic damselfish with five bold black bars on a silvery-white body and a yellow dorsal wash; generally beginner-friendly but tends to become territorial as it matures.
careNotes: The Sergeant Major Damselfish (Abudefduf saxatilis) is an Atlantic and Caribbean species that is often sold alongside — and sometimes confused with — its close Indo-Pacific relative Abudefduf vaigiensis; the two share nearly identical husbandry requirements, but Atlantic-collected fish are A. saxatilis. The species is widely considered hardy and beginner-friendly, though it has been reported to grow noticeably more territorial and aggressive as it matures, particularly toward conspecifics and other damselfish. It is best kept singly in moderate tanks, or in groups of five or more in larger systems where aggression can be diffused across multiple territories. The Sergeant Major is generally considered reef-safe and will not harm corals or sessile invertebrates, but may pester smaller mobile invertebrates and tends to outcompete shyer tankmates at feeding time.
breedingNotes: The Sergeant Major is an egg-layer that forms distinct breeding pairs. Males establish and aggressively defend nesting sites on rocks or reef substrate; females deposit large adhesive egg masses — around 200,000 eggs have been documented in field observations — which the male guards and aerates for approximately a week until hatching. Breeding in home aquaria is uncommon and largely limited to very large, well-established systems.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Collection root used because the direct product page (sergeant-major-damselfish-1) lists this species under the Indo-Pacific congener A. vaigiensis; husbandry values (75 gal min, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, omnivore, semi-aggressive, reef-safe) were extracted from the listing.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, native Atlantic range, habitat, max adult size (22.9 cm TL, common 15 cm SL), temperature envelope (19.9-28.1 C), diet composition, and reproduction. FishBase confirms strictly-Atlantic distribution and notes Indo-Pacific A. vaigiensis as the replacement congener.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Pomacentridae), order (Blenniiformes), max size (22.9 cm), depth range, diet, reproduction (males guard ~200,000 eggs for ~1 week), and IUCN status (Least Concern).

additional.name: Hobbyist sources (aggregated web search)
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank-size spread: LiveAquaria 75 gal / 284 L, TankTag 55 gal / 208 L, saltwaterfish.com 40 gal / 150 L; biased larger to 300 L per playbook tank-size rule given active-swimmer and territorial prose. Lifespan reported as 10+ years.

fish.breedingNotes: An egg-layer with demersal nesting behavior; males guard adhesive clutches of around 200,000 eggs on rock substrate for approximately a week. Rarely bred in home aquaria.
